data.title: Petrobras: Gigantic Yield, It's Time To Be Greedy
data.description:
Article Thesis

Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (PBR) (PBR.A) reported its most recent quarterly earnings results last week, showing a strong operating performance and good financial results despite a weak oil price environment. The company also announced its dividends for the third quarter, with the yield standing at 11% for PBR and at 12% for PBR.A. The dividend story thus remains intact, even with oil prices (CL1:COM) at a lowish level, which is why I consider Petrobras a good income investment.

Past Coverage

I have written about Petrobras here on Seeking Alpha in the past, with this being my most recent article on the company, in which I gave Petrobras a "Buy" rating. Shares have gained 7% since then, outperforming the broad market's 1% return over the same time frame. With Petrobras releasing its most recent earnings results last week and with the company announcing its dividends for Q3—many investors had worried about the sustainability of PBR's high dividend yield—it's time to update my views on the company today.

Petrobras' Quarterly Performance

Last Friday, Petrobras announced its quarterly earnings results for the third quarter, with the slides being available here. As an E&P-focused energy major, Petrobras, unsurprisingly, is impacted by oil price movements to a large degree. Recently, oil prices have been relatively weak, as we can see in the following chart:

Over the last year, Brent (CO1:COM) prices dropped from around the mid-$70s to the mid-$60s, with two very short spikes to around $80 and two very short periods of oil trading in the $50s. As the above chart shows, Q3 2025 prices were rather weak—weaker than in Q3 2024.

Factors for the rather low oil prices we have seen lately are overproduction worries, subdued economic growth in many markets, especially Europe, and easing tensions in the Middle East. While consumers are, of course, happy about lower oil prices, Petrobras and other oil producers see a negative impact on their revenues and profits. Despite this headwind, however, Petrobras performed quite well during the third quarter, which was largely the result of very strong operational execution:

The company was able to hit a new production record (arguably doing its part in fueling overproduction worries by doing so), with production rising to 3.14 million barrels of oil equivalent per day. That was up by a very strong 17% compared to one year earlier, with production also rising by a pretty sizeable 8% on a sequential basis. Many oil companies, especially the supermajors such as Chevron (CVX), BP (BP), or Exxon Mobil (XOM), are slow-growing; thus, Petrobras' production growth rate in the high-teens range stands out versus its peer group. The strong production growth rate was made possible by production increases in major growth areas for the company, especially in the Buzios ultra-deepwater oil field, where one of Petrobras' FPSOs, Almirante Tamandaré, hit a 225,000 barrel per day level in Q3, with that number rising further in October—Q4 results could thus be pretty good as well, I believe.

Management also stated that increased production from fields that have already been operated for a while was a tailwind -- the company became more efficient and found ways to improve rates at older assets. I like this a lot, as this should be relatively high-margin production that doesn't require a lot of additional capital expenditures.

Thanks to the big production increase during the third quarter, which was largely the result of Petrobras' increased spending on its deepwater assets in recent years, Petrobras was able to (mostly) offset the headwinds from lower oil prices during the quarter. Adjusted EBITDA totaled $12.0 billion during the most recent quarter, which was actually up by a couple of percentage points versus Q3 2024, while adjusted net income declined slightly, from $5.5 billion one year ago to $5.2 billion during this year's third quarter. Considering oil prices, based on a quarterly average, pulled back by close to 15%, pretty stable earnings are a great result, I think. This also positions Petrobras pretty well for an eventual oil price increase—I think that it is unlikely that oil will continue to trade where it trades at right now forever.

Whether due to geopolitical tensions or due to improving economic growth, oil prices should improve at some point, and Petrobras should then see a nice profit increase thanks to its strong production growth and ongoing investments in new production in Buzios, the Foz de Amazonas region, and so on.

Operating cash flows were down compared to one year earlier, but were still pretty strong in absolute terms, at $9.9 billion, or almost $40 billion annualized—for a company that's valued at around $82 billion today, that's very impressive, I believe. Capital expenditures totaled $5.5 billion in Q3, leaving $4.4 billion in free cash flow. While some investors have been worrying that Petrobras would ramp up its investments in potentially unprofitable green energy assets due to political pressures, it remains very focused on its core competence—oil production. 85% of Petrobras' investments in Q3 were in exploration and production, with 11% going to the oil downstream business, i.e., refining and marketing.

At least so far, Petrobras does not spend significant amounts of money on green assets, so these worries seem overblown to me. $4.4 billion in free cash flow for the quarter means an annualized free cash flow yield of a little north of 20%—outstanding relative to other major energy companies, and making for a very low valuation, as Petrobras trades with a sub-5x free cash flow multiple even during a rather weak oil price environment.

Petrobras' Dividend Story Remains Intact

Fears about growing green investments at Petrobras went hand in hand with fears about its dividend, but it looks like those were overblown as well. Petrobras announced its dividends for the third quarter, with a per-share payout of 0.472 Brazilian Real, equal to $0.18.

Since PBR and PBR.A, as they are traded on U.S. stock exchanges, equal two shares of Petrobras, investors will receive $0.36 per share in total for the third quarter, divided into two payments that will be made in February and March. With PBR trading at $13.10 and with PBR.A trading at $12.40, the quarterly yield is 2.7% and 2.9%, respectively—annualized, that's a yield of around 11% for PBR and around 12% for PBR.A.

Even during a phase when Petrobras invests significant sums of money into production growth while oil prices are rather low, the company thus offers a double-digit yield. Despite the high dividend yield, Petrobras' dividend doesn't look very risky—the payout ratio isn't very high, at around 55% to 60%, considering the company's free cash flow yield of around 20%. Petrobras looks like a more appealing dividend pick compared to other oil majors:

The Western supermajors offer yields of 3.3% to 5.6%, which is around one-third of what one can get from Petrobras right now. Petrobras has more Brazil exposure, but I don't think that's a major risk any longer—Lula has now been president for a while, and Petrobras keeps doing well, so worries about left-wing leadership in Brazil may have been overblown.

Takeaway

Petrobras' growth investments are paying off—despite rather weak oil prices during the third quarter, the company saw its profits remain relatively flat, as strong production growth and efficiency gains offset oil price headwinds.

Cash generation remained strong as well, which is why Petrobras decided to offer a nice dividend payment for the third quarter—the forward yield is around 11% to 12%, making Petrobras a very high-yielding income stock. Petrobras has share price upside potential on top of that, thanks to a very inexpensive valuation and profit growth potential, especially if oil prices were to climb from today's subdued level.

data.title: AI, Efficiency, And Dominance: Why Tencent's Best Years May Still Be Ahead
data.description:
At the end of August 2025, I published my last article about Tencent Holdings Limited (OTCPK:TCEHY) and in the conclusion, I wrote:

The times in which Tencent must be seen as a major bargain are certainly over. Since its lows in late 2022, the stock price almost tripled from about HKD 200 to HKD 600. Nevertheless, I don’t see Tencent as expensive or overvalued as the stock is still trading below its intrinsic value and below its previous all-time high of around HKD 770, which might serve as a potential price target in the next few quarters for the stock.

Tencent clearly remains a “Buy” and although we should not expect the stock to triple again over the next two or three years, expecting a return on investment of at least 10% for Tencent seems more than reasonable. Of course, this does not mean we should preclude setbacks and corrections in the years to come. Tencent’s stock price might certainly correct for several months before the stock can move higher.

Since my last article was published, Tencent gained about 10% (in USD), and this is certainly a solid performance over less than three months. Today, on November 13, 2025, Tencent reported its third quarter results, and in the following article, I will argue that Tencent is still a “Buy”. I will argue why I still think that Tencent is an above-average investment and a good pick for long-term investors. Especially the improvements in efficiency due to AI developments must be seen rather bullish, and Tencent is continuing to report double-digit growth rates.

Quarterly Results

We start by looking at the last quarterly results, which were great once again. For starters, revenue increased from RMB 167.2 billion in Q3/24 to RMB 192.9 billion in Q3/25 – resulting in 15.4% year-over-year growth for the top line. Operating income increased 19.2% year-over-year from RMB 53,333 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 63,554 million in this quarter. And diluted earnings per share increased from RMB 5.644 in Q3/24 to RMB 6.779 in Q3/25 – resulting in 20.1% year-over-year bottom-line growth.

Free cash flow, one of the most important metrics for almost every business, was flat and once again RMB 58.5 billion.

Combined MAUs of Weixin and WeChat continued to increase, but with a low pace. Year-over-year, the number grew 2.3% to 1,414 million, and fee-based subscriptions were stable at 265 million (the same as 12 months earlier). Tencent Video had 114 million subscriptions and maintained its lead in the long-form video industry, and Tencent Music had 126 million subscriptions.

When looking at the three different business segments, each one was contributing to growth, but FinTech and Business Services grew “only” 9.6% year-over-year to RMB 58,174 million, and this segment is remaining the weakest segment for Tencent. VAS (Value Added Services) increased revenue 15.9% year-over-year to RMB 95,860 million, and Marketing Services increased revenue 20.8% year-over-year to RMB 36,242 million. These are currently the two segments driving growth.

Revenue was underpinned by ad spend growth in all major categories and impressions, as well as average eCPM increased year-over-year.

Cheap Power And Issuing Bonds

Recently, it was reported that the Chinese government has increased subsidies to cut the energy bills for some of the country’s largest data centers by half. Especially Chinese tech giants – including ByteDance, Alibaba, and Tencent – have been faced with higher electricity costs. Following the trade war and Beijing’s ban of NVIDIA’s artificial intelligence chips, the Chinese government is encouraging the big tech companies to run their data center operations on chips that are produced by local manufacturers in China (including Huawei and Cambricon). Apparently, these chips are much less energy-efficient than NVIDIA’s AI chips, and many firms are obviously struggling with higher energy costs.

I don’t know if this is good news or not. On the one hand, it is certainly good news for technology companies, as the energy bill is reduced and making the companies more profitable (or the companies are able to invest the cash in some other ways). On the other hand, it is also indicating that China is not as efficient in producing AI chips as the United States, and in the global race for artificial intelligence, this could be a disadvantage for Chinese companies like Tencent.

So far, Tencent is not really competing with companies like OpenAI or Alphabet, or Microsoft, but if this should be the case at some point, Chinese companies might be at a disadvantage. Tencent is still generating most of its revenue in Mainland China, and only about 10% of revenue is generated in international markets. International Games generated RMB 20.8 billion in quarterly revenue and are one of the fastest growing segments for Tencent, with 11% quarter-over-quarter and 43% year-over-year growth, and these are business segments where Tencent is a clear market leader and does not compete with the other major AI companies in the United States.

I don’t know how much Tencent is affected by increasing costs. When looking at the third quarter results, we can’t see any hints that expenses are exploding, and margins improved quarter over quarter. Tencent issued new bonds in September 2025, which was the company’s first bond sale in four years. And obviously, Tencent needs additional cash for the intensifying AI race, but U.S. technology giants are issuing new debt as well. And according to Bloomberg, Tencent raised only 9 billion yuan (about $1.3 billion) with interest rates at 2.6% (five-year), 3% (ten-year), and 3.6% (thirty-year). These are rather low-interest rates and indicate the high-quality business Tencent actually is.

AI And Efficiency

While Tencent was also taking on additional debt to finance the AI race, and increasing energy bills are certainly a problem, it also seems like Tencent is getting more efficient when looking at margins. Year-over-year, gross margin improved from 53% to 56% and operating margin improved from 31.9% in Q3/24 to 33.0% in Q3/25.

And during a recent conference, Tencent indicated that the implementation of an in-house AI-powered platform called WeDev led to a 67% increase in the company’s R&D automation rate. Additionally, more than a quarter of code-related problems are now discovered by AI, which is leading to a reduction of eight hours for fixing each bug.

While we don’t know how much additional revenue Tencent might generate by AI-powered tools and products, these numbers are indicating that Tencent might be able to lower its expenses over time as a sizeable portion of coding tasks might be fulfilled by AI in the future. Tencent also reported that half of all new code written so far in 2025 has been done with the help of AI, which was leading to a 40% drop in the average amount of time that was spent on coding tasks.

But Tencent is also offering its language models and AI-tools as open source (mostly the language models) and as SaaS (several AI tools). In a press release, Tencent wrote:

The upgraded SaaS+AI toolkit enhances office collaboration, including AI Minutes in Tencent Meetings, which has seen a year-on-year growth rate of 150% over the past year. It also supports knowledge management, such as Tencent LearnShare, currently used by over 300,000 enterprises enjoying 92% response accuracy. CodeBuddy, an AI-powered AI coding tool for developers, reduces coding time by 40% and increases R&D efficiency by 16%.

Up until now, Tencent is not seen as one of the major cloud service providers. Nevertheless, more than 90% of leading Chinese internet companies, and 95% of leading Chinese gaming companies, are using Tencent Cloud to support global expansion initiatives. Of course, it remains unclear which companies qualify as “leading”, but we can be optimistic that Tencent is slowly developing into a more important player in the cloud market as well.

Growth

Summing up the growth expectations, we can be quite optimistic that Tencent will continue its double-digit growth path in the years to come. In the case of Tencent, improving margins will contribute to growth in the coming years, as will share buybacks. However, the biggest contributor to overall growth will be top-line growth.

Analysts are expecting revenue to grow with a CAGR of 10.53% in the years between fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2034, which seems reasonable when looking at the growth rates Tencent was reporting not only in the last few quarters but also over the long run.

And these growth rates for revenue might seem reasonable. When combining top-line growth of 10% to 11% with slightly improving margins and share buybacks, Tencent should be able to grow its bottom line at least 12% annually for the years to come.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

A final step in every analysis is to determine whether a stock is undervalued and still a good investment, or if a great business might be overvalued already. When looking at the simple valuation multiples, we can’t make the argument anymore that Tencent is a bargain. At the time of writing, Tencent is trading for 27.2 times earnings. This is still below the long-term average of 33.88, nevertheless, a valuation multiple close to 30 is certainly not indicating a bargain anymore, and a company has to grow with a high pace to justify these valuation multiples.

Instead of the price-earnings ratio, we can look at the price-to-free-cash-flow ratio, which is currently at 19.7. And for a business growing with a high pace, a valuation multiple around 20 can certainly be seen as reasonable and might indicate a stock that is still undervalued. The P/FCF ratio was lower than the P/E ratio most of the time in the last almost 20 years, but I would usually see the P/FCF ratio as the better ratio among the two.

The assessment that Tencent is probably still undervalued can be backed up by using a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we calculate with a 10% discount rate (as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least from our investment). Additionally, we calculate with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (9,232 million in Q3/25).

The trailing twelve months free cash flow was almost the same as in the previous quarter (RMB 153 billion), and we will use that number as the basis once again. And similar to previous articles, we calculate with a growth rate of 12% once again, followed by 6% growth till perpetuity. Calculating with these assumptions leads to an intrinsic value of RMB 633 and HKD 692. At the time of writing, Tencent is trading for HKD 656, and the stock is therefore still slightly undervalued.

Additionally, we can make the argument that the free cash flow we are taking as basis is rather pessimistic, as the number was stagnating or even slightly declining in the last few quarters. We can therefore make the argument for an improving free cash flow conversion, and free cash flow might be rather RMB 175 billion to RMB 200 billion next year. And when using RMB 175 billion as basis, we get an intrinsic value of HKD 791, and Tencent would therefore be trading about 20% below its intrinsic value.

Conclusion

I remain bullish about Tencent in the years to come and confident that the business will continue growing at a high rate (most likely in the double-digits), and investors should be able to achieve at least 10% annual return from an investment.

Tencent is not a major bargain anymore, and compared to other Chinese technology companies like Alibaba or Baidu, it is trading for higher valuation multiples. But Tencent continues to perform with high levels of consistency and is one of the better long-term investments in my opinion. Especially, the high levels of stability and consistency for the business are speaking for Tencent as a long-term investment.

And while it remains unclear if Tencent can actually generate huge amounts of revenue from AI tools in the years to come, it seems like the company found a way to use AI to reduce coding time and therefore expenses, which might have a positive impact on margins in the years to come.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Sealed Air Corporation Pops On Takeover Rumors, Shares Can Still Climb Higher
data.description:
November 13 was a fascinating day for investors in Sealed Air Corporation (SEE). The company, which produces Bubble Wrap, as well as other packaging materials, saw its share price jump around 19.4% after news broke, the day prior, that it is engaging in talks with a private equity firm by the name of Clayton Dubilier & Rice to potentially be taken private. I have been saying for a while now that the stock is meaningfully undervalued. Despite some troubles that the company has experienced from a revenue perspective in recent years, and even though some profitability metrics have also worsened, this is a very cheap and high-quality player. In fact, in my last article about it, published in early October of this year, I argued that other investors were finally starting to see what I had been seeing.

Since then, shares are up 23.5%, which is massively above the 0.5% increase that the S&P 500 has seen over the same period. And since my article before that, published in June of this year, shares are now up 41.3% compared to the 13.4% rise that the market has enjoyed. Although the easy money has probably been had if these rumors turn out to be true, I would argue that further upside is probable. The firm's overall net debt picture makes it a relatively lower-risk prospect. Management has been working hard to cut costs, with the target of capturing annualized savings of between $140 million and $160 million. Those efforts are finally starting to show on the firm's bottom line. And when you add on top of it how shares are priced even after rising so much, I think that this is a very compelling "Buy" candidate.

A turnaround play

I would argue that it would be appropriate to describe Sealed Air Corporation as something of a turnaround play. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. Revenue for the company contracted during this time from $5.64 billion to $5.39 billion. This drop was driven entirely by the Protective segment, which, as its name suggests, focuses on the production and sale of protective packaging products such as its signature Bubble Wrap brand, as well as others. It also has its own Autobag brand bagging systems, certain types of suspension and retention packaging products, and more.

During these last three years, revenue contracted from $2.32 billion to $1.81 billion. This decline was driven by both price drops and lower volume, with management blaming this on a combination of factors. Volumes were the biggest problem, as shown in the chart below. Management said that this was the result of continued market pressures and destocking in the industrial and fulfillment sectors. The price declines, meanwhile, were primarily because of pricing pressures from competitors and the ability of the company to pass on lower raw material costs to its customers in the form of savings. Naturally, this decline in revenue brought with it a decline in profitability for the segment. But the good news is that this weakness was offset to some extent by a strong Food segment.

As its name suggests, the Food segment focuses on selling food related packaging products. From 2022 through 2024, revenue actually jumped from $3.32 billion to $3.58 billion. Overall pricing during this time was virtually flat, though there was a jump in volumes of $136.5 million from 2023 to 2024 that management said could be chalked up to higher-end market demand and competitive share gains in its space. But the big driver, beyond any doubt, was the firm's purchase of Liquibox, which was acquired in 2023. That enterprise added $285 million in revenue that year and another $23.5 million in revenue in 2024.

This year, things have started to pay off. Even though both operating segments of the company experienced weakness, with the Food segment reporting a $2.3 million decline in revenue thanks to lower volumes of $12 million and foreign currency fluctuations of $11.6 million that were offset mostly by a $21.3 million benefit from higher pricing, and even with the Protective segment dropping from $1.36 billion to $1.30 billion as lower pricing and volume from continued end market weakness negatively affected results, overall profitability for the company improved.

In fact, even though revenue declined from $4.02 billion to $3.96 billion for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year, net income jumped from $269.9 million to $396.8 million. Even on an adjusted basis, we got an increase from $349.2 million to $379.7 million. The improvements that the company saw here were driven in part by lower selling, general, and administrative costs. On top of this, interest expense for the company declined as management focused on meaningful debt reduction.

The improvements on the operational side can be chalked up in large part to the firm’s CTO2Grow program, which is a major cost-cutting initiative aimed at reducing expenses by between $140 million and $160 million. This is a process that has already been mostly paid for by the company. And as for the debt reduction, as of the most recent quarter, the company had net debt of $3.89 billion. That compares to the $4.07 billion that it had at the end of last year. That's a total debt reduction of $177.9 million. And in all likelihood, more is expected by the end of this year, seeing as how management has targeted $250 million in debt pay down this year in its entirety.

Although the Protective segment for the company continues to see bottom-line pain, with gross profit declining from $408.7 million to $376.1 million year over year, this was more than offset by the Food segment, which saw a pop from $810.1 million to $824.7 million. And the interest expense reduction was even more impactful in terms of bottom line results, with the decline year over year from $188.9 million to $168 million.

Management recently increased guidance for this year. Previously, they anticipated revenue of between $5.1 billion and $5.5 billion. The midpoint of that is still the same at $5.3 billion, but the lower end of the range has been pushed up to $5.275 billion, while the higher end of the range has been pushed down to $5.325 billion. However, EBITDA for the company is now expected to be between $1.12 billion and $1.14 billion.

That compares to the $1.075 billion to $1.175 billion previously anticipated. The firm was also thinking before that adjusted earnings per share would be between $2.90 and $3.30. But now, that range has been pushed up to between $3.25 and $3.35. At the midpoint, that would mean an adjusted net income of $485.8 million. And in all likelihood, we would be looking at adjusted operating cash flow of around $899.1 million.

In the chart below, you can see what all of this means for the valuation of the company. Focusing on the cash flow metrics, we can see that even after the share price jumped, the business is trading in the high single-digit range. To put this in perspective, in the same chart, I stacked Sealed Air Corporation up against five other similar firms. And even after seeing its share price rise so much, it is still around the middle of the group, with two of the five firms trading cheaper than it is.

Another positive here is that, even if net debt does not decline from this point on, the firm's leverage is certainly under control. In the chart below, you can see these companies compared to our prospect on a net leverage basis. With a net leverage ratio of 3.44, we can see that three of the five enterprises have higher leverage than what Sealed Air Corporation currently has at this time. And if management reduces debt even more, hitting its target for the end of this year, we would be looking at a net leverage ratio of about 3.38.

Takeaway

Given this data, I must say that I am not surprised to see a private equity firm interested in Sealed Air Corporation. A lot of the hard work has already been done by management to improve costs. At some point, the weakness it is seeing will subside. And when that does, that will make things even more attractive. Given how cheap shares are, even if a deal does not materialize, I would say that Sealed Air Corporation is worth considering. And I would not be shocked at all if any buyout is done at a materially higher price than what the firm is currently trading for.

data.title: Day One Biopharmaceuticals: Mersana Acquisition Ignites March Towards Unmet Need
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Day One Biopharmaceuticals, Inc. (DAWN), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Day One Biopharmaceuticals: NDA Filing Decision Is An Inflection Point To Watch." With respect to this article, I noted the opportunity that this company had whereby its drug tovorafenib was to be reviewed for potential U.S. marketing approval as a kinase inhibitor for the treatment of patients ages 6 months and older with relapsed/refractory pediatric low-grade glioma (pLGG) who harbor the BRAF fusions or rearrangement, or BRAF V600 mutation.

As an update, I'm happy to report that since then, Day One has been able to obtain FDA approval of this drug, now marketed as OJEMDA, to treat these patients with this type of pediatric brain cancer. This company is doing very well at the moment because, since then, it has been able to continue to grow its revenues for this drug. Consider that in the most recently reported Q3 of 2025, it noted that it generated OJEMDA net product revenue of $38.5 million. This was a quarter-over-quarter growth percentage increase of 15%.

Secondly, this company is already working its way towards expanding the possible patient population it can go after in terms of pLGG. How so? Well, the reason why is because it is in the middle of evaluating OJEMDA monotherapy in the phase 3 FIREFLY-2, treating patients with RAF mutation (altered) pLGG who required front-line systemic therapy. The point of this program is that the company is on track to complete enrollment from this FIREFLY-2 study in the 1st half of 2026. The only downside with this program is that the company has marketing rights for this program in the United States, but every other territory outside of this has rights given to Ipsen.

I had a Strong Buy rating when I last wrote about this, and I believe it is important to keep this rating. The reason why is because of the huge news of the day, which is that Day One is paying $25 per share of cash to acquire Mersana Therapeutics (MRSN).

This is only the initial part of the deal, as this transaction also includes the addition of a contingent value right (CVR) of up to $30.25. If everything goes well with both parts of the deal, then the total equity value of the agreement could reach $55.25 per share in cash. The reason for this merger agreement was so that Day One could get its hands on Emi-Le (XMT-1660), an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) directed against B7-H4-expressing solid tumors. With the first program already explored in a phase 1 study, using this ADC to target patients with adenoid cystic carcinoma type-1 (ACC-1).

With sales of OJEMDA growing well, along with a possible expansion opportunity towards 1st-line RAF-altered pLGG, plus the acquisition agreement of Mersana Therapeutics to bring on board Emi-Le as a differentiated B7-H4 ADC, I believe that I should keep the Strong Buy rating on Mersana Therapeutics.

Acquisition Of Emi-Le Makes Sense From A Strategic Standpoint

As I noted above, Day One Biopharmaceuticals announced that it had entered into a merger agreement to acquire Mersana Therapeutics. Initially, the deal is to be valued at $25 per share at closing, which represents an equity market value of $129 million. Of course, this then includes the contingent value right (CVR) of $30.25 in cash that has been set forth as well. Again, if all of the expected milestones for the entire CVR go through, then this would value the total deal at $285 million (total $55.25 per share in cash). The truth is that the CVR is split up a lot, and thus, this CVR is not going to happen immediately. It is going to be split according to clinical milestones and then regulatory/sales-based milestones. With that being said, the intended clinical milestones in place are as follows:

  • Development milestone of existing partnership agreement - eligible to earn CVR of $1.25 per share.
  • Breakthrough Therapy Designation granted for the ACC indication by FDA - eligible to earn CVR of $1.00 per share.
  • Dosing of first patient in a registrational trial using Emi-Le for the treatment of patients with ACC-1 - eligible to earn $4.00 per share.

Then, there is the other aspect to consider, which is regulatory/sales-based milestones. There are a total of 6 other CVRS to be earned with this category. However, the largest one to mention would be the regulatory approval of Emi-Le for the treatment of patients with ACC-1 by the FDA. If this happens, then the CVR to be earned for this would be $9.00 per share. The last item to mention relating to this deal is that, of course, it has to make it through rigorous regulatory scrutiny before this deal can be finalized. If all goes well in terms of closing conditions, then it is expected to close by the end of January 2026.

The thing is that the ability to acquire Mersana Therapeutics makes sense from two strategic vantage points. The first vantage point is that Day One's pipeline is built on targeting cancers with an unmet medical need. With this merger deal in place, it obtains Emi-Le, which is a differentiated antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) that targets B7-H4 solid tumors. One of the first programs that Mersana was working on, before it was acquired, was the development of Emi-Le to treat patients with adenoid cystic carcinoma type-1 (ACC-1).

What made this a strong program, and likely why Day One chose to throw the development of Emi-Le into its pipeline, is that there was proof-of-concept established in a phase 1 study. That is, there was antitumor activity noted in an early-stage study using this particular drug to treat these patients. There was first the release of positive data in January 2025, but then the company provided an update of additional results that were released as an oral presentation at the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) 2025 program. This was ideal data because it was noted that there was a 31% (8 out of 26) confirmed objective response rate (ORR) across all solid tumor types of B7-H4 expression.

The point here is that the goal of this company is to shift a focus towards solid tumors that have high expression of this protein. This is a biomarker that can be used to recruit patients who are likely to respond to treatment with Emi-Le. For instance, this specific group of patients who obtained a confirmed ORR of 31% had a B7-H4 tumor proportion score of 70% or higher. This alone is good enough, but what is even better is that the ORR percentage went up as the prior lines of therapies these patients took increased. When looking at patients who received ≤4 prior lines of therapy, their ORR increased to 44%.

What I went over above was just how the data was viewed in a positive manner. Another aspect to consider is the unmet medical need for patients with adenoid cystic carcinoma type-1 (ACC-1). Consider that at the present time, there are no FDA-approved therapies for these patients. In addition, there is no clearly defined standard of care (SOC) therapy for them either.

To this end, the belief by Day One is that it could shift towards a potential registration study immediately because of this. That is, there might be a quick registration path available for it to take in the development of this drug targeting these ACC-1 patients. If it can gain adequate guidance from the FDA, then Emi-Le might have a quick pathway towards U.S. marketing approval for these patients.

It is my opinion that such an acquisition likely wasn't done on the premise of obtaining an ADC for only one indication of ACC-1. What I mean by this is that there are several other solid tumors that have high expression of B7-H4. If you look at the link above with the data Mersana released with the 31% ORR number, it not only saw a positive signal in targeting patients with ACC-1, but also in those who had triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). In terms of TNBC, it was stated that there were positive signals in these patients who had already been treated with a topoisomerase-1 inhibitor. The point is that B7-H4 is highly expressed in many solid tumor types but is limited in healthy tissue. This is what makes the development of this ADC an ideal one.

The other strategic reason why it made sense for Day One to acquire Mersana Therapeutics for Emi-Le was because it already had another ADC in place. This is an ADC directed to target PTK7-expressing solid tumors known as DAY301. Specifically, the company is in the process of running a phase 1a/1b open-label, dose-escalation study using this drug to target patients with locally advanced or metastatic solid tumors. The premise for advancing this is because of preclinical studies going well to target such patients with advanced solid tumors.

Speaking of which, PTK7 targeting is ideal because there are many tumor types that highly express this protein on their cell surface, and they are as follows:

  • Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) which I mentioned above.
  • Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC).
  • Platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (PROC).
  • Osteosarcoma.
  • Neuroblastoma.

The point of this ADC is not just to be a similar drug like others. The goal was to be developed to overcome limitations of other ADCs targeting PTK7. In essence, to potentially become the best-in-class ADC targeting this protein. It is important to state, though, that this candidate wasn't developed by Day One. This is a drug it had licensed from MabCare Therapeutics. Even though this drug is licensed from this entity in China, Day One was able to obtain worldwide exclusive rights to DAY301. The only exclusion is that it holds no rights for this drug in the Greater China region.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Day One Biopharmaceuticals had cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments of $451.6 million as of September 30, 2025. The company had to use its strong cash position in order to acquire Mersana Therapeutics. The company noted that it should have some cash to fund its operations, but the 10-Q SEC Filing before this acquisition notes a risk of needing to raise additional cash. The reason why is because, according to this SEC filing, there was an estimation made that the company of its cash runway that it would only have enough cash on hand to fund its operations at least through the next 12 months.

This means it is my belief at some point in 2026 that it may need to enact some type of cash-raising activity to keep its operations going. With that being said, there is an ATM agreement in place that it could utilize. This is an Equity Distribution Agreement, it had entered into with Piper Sandler & Co. and JonesTrading Institutional Services LLC as sales agents. This was done so that shares of common stock could be issued and sold with an aggregate offering price of $250 million. The good news to note about this is that as of September 30, 2025, no shares have been sold under this agreement. This biotech is different than most others for the simple reason that it is already generating revenues with an approved drug.

This drug, known as OJEMDA has already been approved by the FDA to treat patients 6 months or older with relapse/refractory pediatric low-grade glioma (pLGG) who harbor a BRAF fusion or rearrangement or a BRAF V600 mutation. Sales of OJEMDA in Q3 of 2025 reached $38.5 million, which was a growth percentage of 15% from the prior Q2 of 2025.

The company burns roughly $59.5 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $31.4 million in R&D expenses and then $28.1 million in SG&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Day One Biopharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be in terms of this latest merger agreement with Mersana Therapeutics. The risk here is that even though there has been sufficient antitumor activity observed in a phase 1 study using Emi-Le to treat these patients with adenoid cystic carcinoma type-1 (ACC-1), there is no assurance that this will be observed in other studies. The second risk to consider here would be in terms of the belief by the company that the unmet medical need of this indication (no FDA-approved therapies) could mean a quicker registration pathway for marketing approval. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the FDA is going to allow a quicker pathway route for FDA approval of Emi-Le for the treatment of these patients with ACC-1.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of its net product revenues of OJEMDA, which I went over above. As I stated, quarter-over-quarter growth for net product revenues of this drug was 15% to $38.5 million. The risk here is that despite achieving this sales outcome, and the company raising its full-year 2025 revenue guidance for OJEMDA, there is no guarantee that its sales expectations over the next several quarters are going to be met.

The third risk to consider would be in terms of DAY301 as a PKT7-targeted ADC being developed to treat patients with solid tumors. The risk here is that the concept of mode of action of this ADC targeting tumors has only been established in preclinical testing. This means there is no assurance that the data that has been generated in preclinical testing is going to be shown again in phase 1 human testing. The hope is that it can advance this candidate because it worked hard to obtain worldwide exclusive rights for DAY301, targeting solid tumors that express PKT7.

The fourth risk to consider here would be in terms of the merger agreement deal that Day One Biopharmaceuticals made with Mersana Therapeutics. The reason why is because this particular deal, to acquire this company and Emi-Le is still contingent upon customary closing conditions and other regulatory laws. If all goes well, the transaction is expected to close at the end of January 2026.

The first risk here is that there is no assurance that this deal will be able to get through all satisfactory closing conditions without a problem. A second risk to consider would be in terms of the CVR of $30.25 per share in cash. The reason why is because this portion of the deal is tied to several separate milestones that must be met. Thus, the risk here is that there is no guarantee that the CVR laid out will be partially or fully attained over time. A lot of the milestones might be rough to achieve. For instance, the CVR of $9.00 per share tied to Emi-Le FDA approval is not a guaranteed outcome. If a rejection of approval happens, then shareholders would not be entitled to receive that specific CVR.

The fifth and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that this company finds itself in. In the press release, it notes that its vigorous cash position allowed it to acquire Mersana Therapeutics as part of the initial $25 per share amount. However, in the 10-Q SEC Filing it released on November 4, 2025, it notes in there that it believes that its cash on hand will only be enough to fund its operations at least through the next 12 months. This means it will either have to find a way to raise funds through a financial transaction in 2026, or it may have to tap into the ATM agreement I noted above that it had made with Piper Sandler & Co. and JonesTrading Institutional Services LLC as sales agents.

Conclusion

I believe that Day One Biopharmaceuticals made the right move to acquire Mersana. The reasons why it was a good idea, I have gone over above. For starters, it brings in an ADC that targets B7-H4, which is expressed in many solid tumors but not expressed much, if at all, in healthy tissues. This would be Emi-Le, which has already shown antitumor activity in treating patients with adenoid cystic carcinoma type-1 (ACC-1). The premise for this program is that there are no FDA-approved therapies for ACC-1; thus, this is a good target indication to go after because of that. Plus, there might be an expedited registrational pathway of FDA approval as well. Of course, that's pending there are positive discussions with the U.S. FDA for it to obtain such an accelerated approval pathway.

The beauty of Day One obtaining Emi-Le through the Mersana Therapeutics acquisition is that it is not just to be applied to the targeting of patients with ACC-1. It is that it can be utilized for a variety of other cancers. Thus, the acquisition makes sense from a strategic point of potentially obtaining approval for an indication that is considered to be an unmet medical need. Plus, the fact that it already had another ADC in its pipeline being developed by the company as well, known as DAY301. With everything mentioned above, plus the potential to obtain Emi-Le as an ADC targeting B7-H4-expressing solid tumors, I believe that I should keep the Strong Buy rating on this stock.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Michael Burry Waves Bye
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Burry wound up his fund just days after disclosing bets against AI trade. (0:15). Disney tumbles on TV, film weakness. (1:55) AI trade could kill buybacks. (3:39)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, from Big Short to Big Mystery.

Michael Burry has wound up his hedge fund, Scion Asset Management, just days after making waves by betting against the AI trade.

Scion terminated its registration with the SEC effective November 10. In a letter to investors dated October 27 and confirmed by the Financial Times, Burry wrote, “With a heavy heart, I will liquidate the funds and return capital—minus a small audit and tax holdback—by year’s end. My estimation of value in securities is not now, and has not been for some time, in sync with the markets.”

He added that investors could reach out to associate PM Phil Clifton about his next move. But on X, Burry teased “much better things November 25.”

Last week, Scion disclosed bearish positions in Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR). The man who famously predicted the 2008 housing crisis has recently been warning about inflated AI valuations and creative accounting at hyperscalers. He argues that cloud and AI infrastructure firms are boosting earnings by extending the “useful life” of their assets—what he calls one of the most common modern accounting tricks.

Burry estimates the practice could understate depreciation by $176 billion between 2026 and 2028, overstating profits at Oracle (ORCL) and Meta (META) by more than 20%.

You can check out a full timeline of Burry’s Wall Street arc on Seeking Alpha — where, by the way, we’re having a Black Friday sale with 20% off all products.

In today’s trading, investors are echoing Burry’s pessimism about tech – for this session at least.

The Nasdaq (COMP.IND) is the weakest among the major averages, down 2% in afternoon trading. The S&P (SP500) and Dow (DJI) are off more than 1%.

Some of the biggest decliners include Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Tesla (TSLA) and Palantir (PLTR).

Among active stocks, Disney (DIS) is tumbling on weakness in its traditional TV and film businesses in fiscal Q4 – overshadowing growth in streaming and parks.

Bank of America’s Jessica Reif Ehrlich says Disney’s results are likely to lean heavily on the back half of the year.

The first quarter, she says, will be weighed down by about $150 million in pre-opening and dry-dock costs in the Experiences business, a $400 million hit from tough theatrical comparisons, $140 million in lower political ad revenue, and a $73 million drag from Star India.

On the bright side, Disney announced a 50% boost to its dividend and plans to double share buybacks to $7 billion in fiscal 2026.

Verizon (VZ) — under new CEO leadership promising cost cuts and a turnaround — plans to lay off roughly 15,000 workers and transition about 200 stores into franchises.

The telecom has lost postpaid phone subscribers for three straight quarters as competition intensifies in both home internet and mobile service.

And Firefly Aerospace (FLY) is soaring after a strong earnings report and a Deutsche Bank upgrade to Buy.

Analyst Edison Yu says a fix for the Alpha rocket’s first-stage engine issue gives investors better visibility into Flight #7 — expected late this year or early 2026.

In other news of note, Target (TGT) is hoping a new staff policy will bring shoppers closer — literally.

USA Today says the policy - called the 10-4 program - requires employees within 10 feet of shoppers to smile, make eye contact and wave. If shoppers come closer, within 4 feet, employees must greet them and initiate a warm helpful interaction.

Chief Stores Officer Adrienne Costanzo says: "Heading into the holiday, we're making adjustments and implementing new ways to increase connection during the most important time of the year."

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, the race for AI dominance could soon wipe out one of corporate America's favorite avenues for shareholder return: the stock buyback.

The market may be on the brink of a “de facto Fed tightening,” according to Nomura strategist Charlie McElligott. If growth and inflation reaccelerate — a scenario he says would catch most investors flat-footed — then loose financial conditions could create “such a profound wealth effect via the stock market” that traders would have to price in a less dovish Fed path.

“In that kind of backdrop,” he says, the AI theme — which is the stock market — begins to lean on credit spreads to keep chasing capex, while free-cash-flow burn hits the cash that’s funded buybacks for the past 15 years.

In other words, hello data centers, bye bye buybacks.

data.title: HUYA: Eyes On Positive Revenue Growth And First Meaningful Operating Earnings
data.description:
I remain a bull on HUYA Inc. (HUYA). The stock is trading at below two-thirds of its forward one-year revenue. Its solid 3Q25 results are supportive of a re-rating.

My prior August 18, 2025, write-up touched on its share repurchases and 2Q25 outperformance.

Financial Improvement Leads To Optimism About 2026 Prospects

HUYA's 3Q25 financials were disclosed in a 6-K filing published on Nov. 12.

Its turnover had contracted in 12 of the 15 preceding quarters on YoY terms.

In the Jul-Sep period this year, the firm's topline rose +1.4% to CNY 1.69 billion.

This is the third quarter in a row of positive growth for HUYA.

Its quarterly sales didn't surpass the CNY 1,670 million mark for the past two years prior to 3Q2025.

HUYA is benefiting from external and internal tailwinds.

There is increased regulatory clarity for Chinese live-streamers. The policymakers also appear to be more accommodating towards the domestic media sector.

State media "Global Times" reported in mid-2025 that "a (new) draft regulation for supervising live-streaming e-commerce" outlined "responsibilities for various players in the sector, including platform operators, live-stream hosts."

Going forward, HUYA will be in a better position to understand what can or cannot be done to monetize its platform users.

My Nov 3, 2025, piece on iQIYI (IQ) mentioned a relaxation of "regulatory constraints for the (Mainland China) long-form drama industry."

It's reasonable for me to assume that China's authorities are gradually loosening their grip on media-related content and channels to some degree.

Consequently, HUYA's "live streaming" top line increased 3% YoY for 3Q25. This segment had previously suffered from negative growth in every single quarter since 4Q2021.

Separately, the efforts to optimize its revenue structure are showing good results.

"Non-streaming" or "game-related" businesses made up 31% of HUYA's group-level sales in the recent three-month timeframe. The same metric was lower at 26% and 27% in 2Q25 and 3Q24, respectively.

Its 6-K filing credited the increased diversification to higher "in-game item sales," as the enterprise "deepened and expanded collaborations with game partners."

HUYA saw its 3Q2025 gaming-related turnover jump 30% to CNY 0.53 billion on a YoY basis.

It guided at the analyst briefing that "we expect overall revenue growth to accelerate (in 2026) versus 2025."

The consensus's current FY25 and FY26 revenue expansion forecasts are 6.7% and 6.5%, respectively, as per S&P Capital IQ. This sets the stage for a potential beat in the following year.

Its results call commentary noted the "upcoming launch of our first title, Goose Goose Duck Mobile, a social deduction game." HUYA described it "as a key step in our strategy to diversify into game publishing."

The non-streaming operations have further upside potential in the form of "game publishing", which should be a growth engine for '26. Earlier, it was primarily focused on "games advertising," and "in-game item sales" highlighted above.

Q3 Marked An Operating Profitability Inflection Point

HUYA has been loss-making at the EBIT level for FY23/24 and 1Q25.

It registered a marginal 2Q2025 "operating profit" or "OP" of CNY 350k.

A breakthrough was finally realized in the latest quarter with an OP amounting to CNY 6.31 million.

At its Q3 call, HUYA indicated, "we expect to see gradual margin improvement over time" as "we further scale and expand our game-related services."

Its 3Q25 EBIT-to-sales was a modest 0.4%.

My projection is that HUYA's operating margin can possibly go up to the low-single-digit percent level in '26.

I infer from management's above-mentioned remarks that its non-streaming division is capitalizing on "economies of scale" in tandem with a growing revenue base.

HUYA is also getting leaner.

The group's "Sales & Marketing/S&M" and "Research & Development/R&D" costs declined by 4% and 3% to CNY 0.07 billion and CNY 0.12 billion, respectively, for 3Q2025.

HUYA's top line grew, despite spending less in key business areas. This was attributed to "decreased staff costs as a result of enhanced efficiency" and "decreased channel promotion fees" at the results meeting.

Also, HUYA has consistently achieved positive quarterly normalized net profit during the 1Q24-3Q25 period.

The firm's 3Q2025 earnings of CNY 36 million were 53% lower YoY. But this isn't something to be worried about. The difference is driven by a 64% plunge in "interest income." It distributed "a special dividend of $1.08 per share ($0.25 billion)" late last year, which shrunk its cash balance.

You Would Pay Attention To These Risks

HUYA's future margins will take a hit if it decides to invest recklessly without regard for profitability.

The company's revenues might be under pressure, assuming the live-streaming and games industries in China see adverse regulatory changes.

My Closing Remarks

HUYA is still deserving of a "Buy" rating. I'm impressed with its solid 3Q25 showing. The shares are undemanding with an FY26 "price-to-sales" of 0.64x (source: S&P Capital IQ).

My target P/S for this name is 0.87x, pegged to its three-year historical mean. This implies a capital appreciation potential of 36% (0.87/0.64).

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Long-Term Brand Dominance At A Bargain
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Marco Franciamore as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Intro and Thesis

In my 11 years as an investor, my most lucrative strategy has been buying companies with strong moats, low debt, dealing with a bipolar Mr. Market in a depressive state — to borrow from Ben Graham. While Novo Nordisk S/A's (NVO) potential turnaround may not mirror the spectacular rebounds seen in Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) or Netflix, Inc. (NFLX), owning a market leader at these lower valuations can still generate attractive returns and offer valuable diversification, especially in a market where many stocks are priced to perfection. Novo fits Buffett’s definition of a “wonderful company at a fair price.” Since it’s coming from a period of prosperity (which often brings waste), the company has many resources to boost earnings, cash flows and so this plunged stock price: the CVS deal, cost-cutting, global dominance and better margins than competitors are just some of them. The main risk I see is the 2032 U.S. semaglutide patent expiration, but Novo’s pipeline—second to none—should be well positioned to absorb the cliff.

A Brief History of Novo

Novo Nordisk starts its story in the 1920s, just after scientists succeeded in manufacturing insulin for the first time. Since the beginning, diabetes has been its focus: the company will be steadily among the world’s largest producers of insulin, and from the 2010s it’s the biggest one (producing almost half of it). In the early 2000s it makes the revolutionary discovery of semaglutide, that FDA will approve only in December 2017.

This new drug, belonging to the GLP-1 agonist’s family, will initially serve the only purpose of lowering blood sugar levels more effectively, and with just one injection per week. But, Novo started to notice the added benefit of reducing body weight: so, in June 2021, semaglutide obtains FDA’s approval for weight loss. While it’s the same drug (semaglutide), Novo markets it as Ozempic for diabetics and as Wegovy for the obese. Patients on the highest dose lose, on average, 15% of their body weight.

Dissecting the ~70% Slump And Competitive Analysis

Since 2018 the growth of Ozempic has been astronomical, becoming soon the main bulk of the company’s revenue. For FY24 the drug has arrived at more than 40% of total sales.

Despite this growth, Novo’s leadership in diabetes has been challenged by the mid-2022 approval of Mounjaro, Eli Lilly’s GLP-1 (and GIP) drug. This drug is similar in many ways to Novo’s product — from side effects to the once-a-week injection — but its active ingredient, tirzepatide, leads to a greater percentage of weight loss. Indeed the higher tirzepatide dose (approved as Zepbound, LLY’s obesity drug) makes you lose on average 21% of your weight. Despite everyone knowing LLY’s superiority from June 2022 (SURMOUNT-1 publication), NVO shares went up more than 150% from that moment—surpassing the $142 per share.

While the stock rise may have been overblown, the growth was sustained by its strong improvement in fundamentals. Furthermore, in the meanwhile Novo’s semaglutide has obtained key FDA’s approvals: on March 2024 for its cardiovascular benefits (reducing “the risk of cardiovascular events like heart attacks or strokes by 20%”), on January 2025 for chronic kidney disease, and lastly on August 15th for MASH (a severe form of fatty liver disease, that affects 14.9 million people only in US). In these areas, Novo is still superior to Lilly — which has no FDA approvals yet. Actually, LLY’s tirzepatide will likely gain these approvals as well—the first two likely by 2027 and the one for MASH about a year later. But recent studies suggest Novo’s drugs have the edge: Wegovy cuts the risk of heart attack, stroke or death by 57% versus Mounjaro and Zepbound.

Going back to the NVO price chart, shares started to tumble when the theoretical superiority of Lilly became factual in economic numbers and especially when the graph of more US prescriptions of Zepbound (against Wegovy) started to scare Novo investors.

Q2 results were striking not only because Zepbound sales surpassed Wegovy, but also because Mounjaro outpaced Ozempic in the diabetes segment: the first sold indeed ≈$5.2 billion against the ≈$5 billion (31.8 billion DKK) of the NVO’s drug.

This dynamic can depress investors but let’s take a more rational perspective. First, in H1 2025 NVO had again a strong improvement in YoY sales (+16.14%) and profits (+22.17%). FCF (-18.7%) is the main negative note, but it’s justified by an almost 50% increase in CapEx. That’s the welcomed scale-up in API and fill-finish sites. From here the FCF should keep growing, since “in the coming years, the capital expenditure to sales ratio is […] expected to be low double digits” while revenue, profit and OCF continue to rise. Despite this temporary drop, the TTM FCF is already massive: the margin to sales is 20% and in the last 10 years it has been around 29%; and we are still in a reasonable EV/FCF=22.61.

Before estimating how much this FCF can grow (and so going to the DCF), let’s dive into the market prospects of the sector.

GLP-1 Market Forecasts

Forecasts from different sources like Grand View Research, PWC and UBS expect the GLP-1 market to more than double by 2030. It is a CAGR of 17.46%, and the trend is expected to continue going forward.

This growth will come from both the diabetes and obesity segments. Despite the fact that this will hurt the insulin market, the latter is still expected to grow at a reasonable 3.7%, which is a good line of defense for almost 19% of Novo’s revenues. But since almost all the remaining 81% comes from GLP-1 drugs (75% specifically), we should find out if Novo has enough fire power to exploit at least part of the strong growth forecasted in the industry.

When analyzing the GLP-1 market, many forget that Novo is still the dominant player: it has better margins than Lilly, more market share and even a stronger brand (due to both brand awareness and FDA’s approvals discussed before). The chart above shows well this dominance: Novo not only started with more market share but gained it at least until Q125. The main reason for this nearly two-to-one advantage over LLY is that Novo’s flagship drug, Ozempic (that makes alone roughly 40% of Novo’s revenue), generated almost twice the sales of its rival Mounjaro in FY24. But even if we took LLY’s Zepbound against Novo’s Wegovy (where we have shown the surpass of Zepbound subscriptions), the revenue of the latter still exceeds the former, with ≈$5,744 billion versus $5,693 billion in H1. Add to that the minor contributions of GLP-1 drugs as Saxenda and Rybelsus: overall, in the three years shown in the chart, Novo Nordisk has nearly tripled its market.

Catalysts To Counter Growth Threats

Many believe this growth is under threat, but I feel that the fears are overblown. Two consecutive profit warnings might not be pleasant, but the second one seems to me more of a strategy to make the job of the new CEO a fair amount easier. Indeed there are some short-term catalysts that can help keep alive this growth.

First, in the chart of Wegovy’s subscriptions against Zepbound, you can notice a spike of the first at the end of July: this is the +33% prescriptions since May 22, when FDA ban on compounded versions of Wegovy took effect. Furthermore, this regaining of market share is just the start. Indeed, many compounders are still copying Novo’s semaglutide just by selling slight variations of it (like by adding vitamins). According to the Wall Street Journal, the compounding market still makes 30% of the US obesity market. Novo has launched more than 130 lawsuits to deal with it.

The other catalyst is the deal reached with CVS, both Caremark and Pharmacy. As for the first, Novo agreed to pay an higher (but undisclosed) rebate to have Wegovy in the list of preferred drugs, while displacing Lilly’s Zepbound. CVS implemented the new formulary changes starting July 1, contributing also to the huge Wegovy prescriptions spike in the month. At the same time, Novo is also tackling the cash-pay market. Now CVS pharmacies are offering Wegovy at a flat $499 price, a strategy aimed at countering compounders and making the drug more affordable for those without insurance coverage. Other players, such as Walmart and Costco, have followed suit—and for Ozempic too (even if it affects just 2% of the US Ozempic buyers, since the other 98% buy it through insurance at $25).

To conclude, the current blockbusters GLP-1 drugs of both Lilly and Novo are extremely similar. I would say that Lilly’s drugs have the edge, but just for the weight loss part. However, in business, the best product doesn’t win necessarily, and things like the CVS Caremark deal, better margins and first mover advantage can make the difference in at least capturing some of that 17.5% CAGR forecasted in the GLP-1 market—surely until the 2032 US patents expire, and possibly after.

What Comes Next: Analysis of the Pipeline

I see no serious threat to the Novo/Lilly duopoly until at least 2032—when the US patent for semaglutide expires. Other pharma companies are far behind in their drug approval process. Even for the next-gen obesity treatments, Novo and Lilly still have the upper hand.

Despite not achieving the desired 25% weight loss, Cagrisema still got a nice 22.7% on average—which would be better than any other obesity drug currently in the market. Furthermore side effects are similar to Wegovy, and Novo is still working to get approvals for heart diseases— a segment where the company aims to carve out a niche. Novo executives expect CagriSema to hit the market in early 2027, pending regulatory approval.

Amycretin seems to cause even greater weight loss—24.3% according to the latest trial—but the drug won’t reach the market before the end of the decade. Even Lilly has a drug in the pipeline with similar weight-loss effects, retatrutide, which is expected to become available a couple of years earlier.

Anyway in the short term, obesity pills will make most of the new product sales. This market is expected to be “a fifth to a third” of the total in 2030, and the jump in sales could be as soon as these drugs are available, exploiting the many potential consumers that want to avoid injections. From a user perspective, Novo’s Wegovy pill seems much better to me than LLY’s: side effects are much lower and weight loss higher (15% vs 12.4%). But unlike orforglipron (Lilly’s pill), oral semaglutide must be taken first thing in the morning—and, most importantly, you have to fast (no liquids, food, or other medications) for at least 30 minutes afterward. For this reason (especially the non-other medications part), not too many physicians have prescribed oral semaglutide for diabetes (Rybelsus) and something similar could happen for obesity. Furthermore, Lilly’s orforglipron is easier (and cheaper) to manufacture at scale, since it’s a small-molecule drug rather than a peptide. For these motives, analysts expect orforglipron to have twice the sales of Novo’s oral Wegovy (and for some the gap could even be higher). Both Lilly and Novo executives expect their pills to launch next year.

Finally, when discussing the pipeline, Cagrilintide deserves our attention. Despite causing only about 12% in weight loss, this Novo’s drug seems to be far better tolerated than any other obesity treatment, meeting the demand of patients who cannot tolerate either semaglutide or tirzepatide. This drug is not expected to be in the market before 2027.

Summing up, Novo Nordisk not only has a strong pipeline in terms of weight-loss efficacy (see Amycretin), but also addresses many of the other patient needs — from oral options to minimizing side effects.

Going into Efficiency Mode

If until now we have focused on the revenue side, there is another way to boost earnings (and so the stock price): the reduction of costs. I’ve started the article citing huge dominant businesses that went through a massive stock crash. Usually, managers feel more legitimate to cut in these kinds of scenarios and break long-made promises in the name of efficiency (and so earnings and cash flow). After the huge crash in Netflix's stock price in 2022, many maneuvers were taken to secure investors. Beyond the classical layoffs (and the password crackdown), they went as far as offering the possibility of having accounts with ads (for the first time, and against previous continuous disclosures) with a lower payment subscription. And everyone will remember when Mark Zuckerberg inaugurated the year of efficiency in 2023. Well Novo, like Meta or Netflix, never had a real problem in their finances considering the extremely low debt in these companies, but the stocks plunged made managers feel more legitimated in cutting and promoting cash-flow generations. Because in times of prosperity, waste is not uncommon.

Going back to NVO, a tougher cost discipline seems to have arrived too. The new CEO has already announced a global hiring freeze—except for business-critical roles—job cuts equivalent to 11.5% of the workforce, with an estimated $1.3 billions saved annually, and a general control of the cost structure of the company. From an investor point, I see these policies as totally positive after years of abundance.

DCF

I’ve run three different DCFs to evaluate Novo, all with the same conservative assumptions and data, except for the FCF growth rate. Above there is the median scenario, with an 8% FCF growth rate. This gives a fair value of $48.29 with a discount rate of 8%. For the bearish scenario (5% growth) it’s $39.27 while for the bullish (10% growth) it’s $55.40. I know these numbers are not that enticing, but I wouldn’t base my view solely on this conservative math, and I lean more towards a bullish view. Novo Nordisk is a market leader in a growth industry, trading currently at just 13.5 TTM P/E—with a price already crushed almost 70% from the heights. Growth industries are not always good, since many are eager to join the party. But I see many more, and far more unreasonable, prices in the market, and I think that buying Novo at these levels can be a good diversification and should be able to give satisfying returns.

Risks

On August 5th, I’ve started a small position in the stock at $47.47 because I like many of the actions taken by the management as the CVS deal, the battle against compounders and the reduction of costs. To me, the sales slowdown has been discounted enough in the share price and Novo has still many weapons to defend its leadership as better margins, strong brand, cost discipline and an impressive pipeline. That said, I will watch closely how the situation evolves in a few years, especially approaching the 2032 semaglutide patent expire. If valid competitors of amycretin (by Novo) and retatrutide (by Lilly) do emerge, I would consider selling my position, because the risk that Novo share price won’t survive the patent cliff would be greater.

For those new to pharma investing, a patent cliff typically drives a steep revenue drop in the formerly protected drug—often around 80% within a couple of years for small-molecule chemicals (e.g., Lipitor), and 60% or a bit more over a similar timeframe for biologic drugs (e.g., Humira). Semaglutide sits between small molecules and biologics in terms of molecular size and replication complexity.

Some analysts expect a 55–60% sales drop in 2033, but I wouldn’t be surprised to see a 70% decline. Since Wegovy, Ozempic, and Rybelsus currently account for about 69.5% of Novo’s total revenue (FY24), a 70% drop in those products would translate into an overall revenue hit of roughly 48.6%. In other words, next-generation drugs would need to deliver at least ~50% of today’s revenue to offset the impact of the patent cliff.

Conclusions

A market leader in a booming industry, trading at value multiples, puts the odds in our favor. Investors, in my view, are being too short-sighted on Novo—a company fully equipped to fight back this downturn both in the short term, through stronger margins, cost discipline and deals like CVS, and in the long term with a pipeline that’s both aggressive (Amycretin) and diversified (oral drugs, Cagrilintide).

While Lilly currently holds the product edge, I expect the crown to return to the Danish emperor in the years ahead—an event that could serve as the key catalyst for this depressed share price. In the medium term, there are no serious challengers to the Novo-Lilly duopoly, yet any prudent investor should keep a watchful eye on how the landscape evolves.

data.title: PGIM Jennison Financial Services Fund Q3 2025 Contributors And Detractors
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the PGIM Jennison Financial Services Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

Quarter

The PGIM Jennison Financial Services Fund advanced but underperformed the 3.4% return of the S&P 1500 Financials index during the quarter. Relative underperformance was largely driven by security selection within capital markets (especially LPL Financial, KKR (KKR), and Marex Group (MRX)), financial services (primarily Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY) and Apollo Global Management (APO)), and banks (Pinnacle Financial Partners (PNFP)). On the upside, our underweight to financial services, an overweight to banks, and out of index exposure to consumer discretionary (Pulte Group) added the most value.

Key Contributors

  • JPMorgan Chase (JPM)
  • Goldman Sachs (GS)
  • Acadian Asset Management (AAMI)

JPMorgan Chase is a U.S.-based multinational investment back and financial services company with a diversified business mix and strong balance sheet. The company reported solid earnings and distinguished itself across several metrics, most importantly margins. JPMorgan’s scale continues to be a competitive advantage with the quality and durability of its business model standing out.

Goldman Sachs has a strong capital base and leading global positions in investment banking, capital markets, trading, and asset management. It is a business with broad reach and durable competitive advantages across multiple product lines. Additionally, we favor their risk management capabilities and seasoned management team. The company reported strong results in the quarter, and we continue to believe that Goldman Sachs will be a major beneficiary of corporate restructurings and Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) activity, along with favorable credit trends.

Acadian Asset Management is the only pure-play publicly traded systematic manager. Given its very low net leverage, ongoing cash generation, and relatively low cash needs, the company continues to re-invest in the business for organic growth and opportunistically return capital to shareholders through buybacks. The stock was higher in the quarter due to continued growth in its fundamentals.

Key Detractors

  • LPL Financial (LPLA)
  • Ryan Specialty Holdings (RYAN)
  • Visa (V)

LPL Financial is a leading and rapidly growing investment advisory firm. The firm has improved its back office and technology infrastructure, making its platform highly attractive for advisors to join and leverage its comprehensive suite of services. The company underperformed in the quarter due to missing expectations on earnings and the delayed profit acceleration from its recent acquisitions.

Ryan Specialty Holdings is an international specialty insurance (primary focus is in excess and surplus insurance (E&S)) firm that provides innovative solutions for brokers, agents, and insurance carriers. Insurance stocks gave-up some of their gains from earlier in the year – where their defensive nature was favored because of the tariff turmoil. The market continued to be more risk-on in the third quarter. We like Ryan’s business model’s growth, management team, risk controls, and market position.

Visa lost value following passage of The Genius Act, which is legislation designed to authorize and encourage the use of Stablecoins. With considerable uncertainty around the use cases for stablecoins, concerns over potential disintermediation of Mastercard’s payment rails weighed on the shares.

data.description:
Market Review

U.S. equity markets delivered robust gains in the third quarter of 2025, fueled by continued momentum in the development of artificial intelligence (AI) and resilient corporate earnings. The Federal Reserve’s largely anticipated decision to cut interest rates by 25 basis points (bps) provided an additional tailwind to markets at the end of the period. Despite policy risks, underlying economic uncertainty, and concerns about valuations, all major indices finished 3Q at record highs, reflecting underlying investor confidence and risk appetite.

The S&P 500 continued its advance in the quarter. Both the Nasdaq and Russell 1000 Growth outperformed the broader market as investors continued to seek out innovative technology and AI exposure. Broadly, the macro backdrop was supportive during the period. The Federal Reserve's Federal Funds rate cut, the first since September 2024, came amidst evidence of a cooling labor market and softer economic data. While more muted, economic indicators remained positive for real Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and consumer spending. Geopolitical tensions ebbed with the resolution of some tariff uncertainty, but sector leadership and market volatility remained sensitive to headlines, especially in rate-sensitive areas and those exposed to global trade.

Technology, led by large-cap constituents, outperformed all other sectors and was the primary contributor to performance. The communications services sector also capitalized on persistent investor enthusiasm for AI and related growth themes and, along with consumer discretionary, posted strong returns. Underpinning this strength, corporate earnings for the broader market rose year over year, beating analyst expectations and providing support for equity valuations.

As we head into the home stretch of the year, potential economic headwinds and lingering policy uncertainties merit watching.

However, the market largely looked through these concerns in the third quarter as the technology-led rally lifted the broader market to historic highs.

For the third quarter, Index performance is as follows: S&P Financials +3.4%; S&P 500 +8.1%; Russell 1000 Growth +10.5%; and Russell 1000 Value +5.3%.

While policy uncertainty and global conflicts remain unresolved, investor optimism has prevailed for the time being. Following the tariff-related low of April 8, the S&P 500 Index saw its fastest rebound in 75 years and hit an all-time high at the end of September.
